title: High frequency low loss magnetic core and method of manufacture
abstract: A high saturation, low loss magnetic material suitable for high frequency electrical devices, including power converters, transformers, solenoids, motors, and other such devices.
claims:
Claims What is claimed: 1) A low loss, high magnetic saturation magnetic material having a saturation magnetization above 1.5T and low core losses at frequencies above 5KHz, said material formed from a plurality of magnetic particles having an average particle size of 20-1500 pm and having a saturation magnetization above 1.5T, said magnetic particles structured and engineered to have low coercivity, said particles including a thermally-stable coating, said thermally- stable coating having a melting point above 350Â°C and a thickness of 5-200 nm, said coated magnetic particles consolidated using rapid heating and pressure into a mass over 90% of theoretical density of the particles to form said low loss, high magnetic saturation magnetic material. 2) The material defined in claim 1, wherein said magnetic particles are iron, iron-silicon, glassy iron, iron-nickel, and/or iron-cobalt alloy. 3) The material as defined in claim 1, wherein said coating includes a high melting point insulator material. 4) The material as defined in claim 2, wherein said coating includes a high melting point insulator material. 5) The material as defined in claim 1, wherein said coating includes PEEK, polyimide, polyphenyl sulfone, polysiloxane, silicone, polysilizane, phenolic, and/or a polymeric material having a use temperature above 280Â°C. 6) The material as defined in any one of claims 2-4, wherein said coating includes PEEK, polyimide, polyphenyl sulfone, polysiloxane, silicone, polysilizane, phenolic, and/or a polymeric material having a use temperature above 280Â°C. 7) The material as defined in claim 1 , wherein said coating includes a semiconducting or other low conductivity material. 

 8) The material as defined in any one of claims 2-6, wherein said coating includes a semiconducting or other low conductivity material. 9) The material as defined in claim 1, wherein said coating includes ferrite, silicon metal, germanium metal, glassy carbon, boron, arsenide, selenide, or other high resistivity metallic or semiconducting material. 10) The material as defined in any one of claims 2-8, wherein said coating includes ferrite, silicon metal, germanium metal, glassy carbon, boron, arsenide, selenide, or other high resistivity metallic or semiconducting material. 1 l) The material as defined in claim 1, wherein said coating includes an inorganic material. 12) The material as defined in any one of claims 2-10, wherein said coating includes an inorganic material. 13) The material as defined in claim 1, wherein said coating includes silica, silicate, silicate glass, BN, BN nanosheets, A1N, Si 3 N 4 , Ti0 2 , titanate, AI2O3, SiOC, SiAlON, aluminate, aluminosilicate, mica, B 2 0 3 , borosilicate, borate glass, ferromagnetic insulator (Fe 3 0 4 , (Mn(Fe 2 0 ) , Zn(Fe 2 0 4 )) or other dielectric oxide or nitride or other inorganic dielectric insulating material. 14) The material as defined in any one of claims 2-12, wherein said coating includes silica, silicate, silicate glass, BN, BN nanosheets, A N, Si N 4 , Ti0 , titanate, Al 2 0 3 , SiOC, SiAlON, aluminate, aluminosilicate, mica, B 2 0 3 , borosilicate, borate glass, ferromagnetic insulator (Fe 3 0 4 , (Mn(Fe 2 0 4) , Zn(Fe 2 0 4 )) or other dielectric oxide or nitride or other inorganic dielectric insulating material. 15) The material as defined in claim 1, wherein said coated particle has at least one dimension in the 0.5-20 pm size range, and a second dimension 10-5000X greater than said first dimension. 

 16) The material as defined in any one of claims 2-14, wherein said coated particle has at least one dimension in the 0.5-20 pm size range, and a second dimension 10-5000X greater than said first dimension. 17) The material as defined in claim 1, wherein the second coating is in the form of a nanosheet to further increase the resistance of the composite as well as protect the insulator during sintering process, and allow for slippage of the particles during consolidation. 18) The material as defined in any one of claims 2- 16, wherein the second coating is in the form of a nanosheet to further increase the resistance of the composite as well as protect the insulator during sintering process, and allow for slippage of the particles during consolidation. 19) The material as defined in claim 1 , wherein the lowering of glass transition temperature of the coating includes allowing the coating to flow and deform during compaction without breaching during sintering process, then protecting the integrity of the coating. 20) The material as defined in any one of claims 2- 18, wherein the lowering of glass transition temperature of the coating includes allowing the coating to flow and deform during compaction without breaching during sintering process, then protecting the integrity of the coating. 21) The material as defined in claim 1 , wherein said material is used as part of power system such as a power converter, power supply, pulse forming network, inverter, rectifier, motor controller system, or as part of a transformer, choke, inductor, or filter circuit. 22) The material as defined in any one of claims 2-20, wherein said material is used as part of power system such as a power converter, power supply, pulse forming network, inverter, rectifier, motor controller system, or as part of a transformer, choke, inductor, or filter circuit. 

 23) The material as defined in claim 21, wherein said power system operates at a frequency above 50 KHz. 24) The material as defined in claim 22, wherein said power system operates at a frequency above 50 KHz. 25) The material as defined in claim 21 , wherein said power system operates at a frequency above 200 KHz. 26) The material as defined in claim 21, wherein said power system operates at a frequency above 500 KHz. 27) The material as defined in claim 1 , wherein said material is used as a power system which includes a wide band gap semiconductor such as a silicon-carbon or a gallium-nitrogen power electronic component. 28) The material as defined in any one of claims 2-26, wherein said material is used as a power system which includes a wide band gap semiconductor such as a silicon-carbon or a gallium-nitrogen power electronic component. 29) A method for forming a low loss, high magnetic saturation magnetic material having a saturation magnetization above 1.5T and low core losses at frequencies above 5KHz, said method comprising: providing a plurality of magnetic particles having an average particle size of 20-1500 pm and having a saturation magnetization above 1.5T, said magnetic particles structured and engineered to have low coercivity; coating said magnetic particles with a thermally-stable coating, said thermally-stable coating having a melting point above 350Â°C and a thickness of 5-200 nm; and 

 consolidating said coated magnetic particles using rapid heating and pressure into a mass having over 90% of theoretical density of said magnetic particles to form said low loss, high magnetic saturation magnetic material. 30) The method as defined in claim 29, wherein said low loss, high magnetic saturation magnetic material is annealed to relieve residual stresses with or without a field to align the easy magnetic axis, said annealing selected from thermal annealing or magnetic field annealing, said thermal annealing performed at a temperature of 500-650Â°C for 0.5-5 hours in an inert atmosphere, said magnetic annealing performed at a temperature of l50-250Â°C for 200-350 seconds. 31) The method as defined in claims 29, wherein said low loss, high magnetic saturation magnetic material is annealed to relieve residual stresses with or without a field to align the easy magnetic axis. 32) The method as defined in claims 30, wherein said low loss, high magnetic saturation magnetic material is annealed to relieve residual stresses with or without a field to align the easy magnetic axis. 33) The method as defined in claim 29, wherein said high rate consolidation process is field- assisted sintering or spark plasma sintering. 34) The method as defined in any one of claims 30-32, wherein said high rate consolidation process is field-assisted sintering or spark plasma sintering. 35) The method as defined in claims 29, wherein said rapid consolidation process includes one or more holding steps or controlled heating steps to debind, degas, react, pyrolize, or otherwise alter the chemistry of the material. 36) The method as defined in any one of claims 30-34, wherein said rapid consolidation process includes one or more holding steps or controlled heating steps to debind, degas, react, pyrolize, or otherwise alter the chemistry of the material. 

 37) The method as defined in claim 29, wherein an environment in the system is controlled during rapid consolidation so as to be a vacuum, a reducing environment, oxidizing environment, nitriding environment, or some other controlled environment. 38) The method as defined in any one of claims 30-36, wherein an environment in the system is controlled during rapid consolidation so as to be a vacuum, a reducing environment, oxidizing environment, nitriding environment, or some other controlled environment. 39) The method as defined in claim 29, wherein a peak temperature during consolidation is selected to be 60-95% of the melting point of the ferromagnetic material, and between 30- 200% of the melting or decomposition point of the insulating coating. 40) The method as defined in any one of claims 30-38, wherein a peak temperature during consolidation is selected to be 60-95% of the melting point of the ferromagnetic material, and between 30-200% of the melting or decomposition point of the insulating coating. 41) The method as defined in claim 29, including the further step of lowering a glass transition temperature of said coating by allowing said coating to flow and deform during compaction without breaching during sintering process, then protecting the integrity of the coating. 42) The method as defined in any one of claims 30-40, including the further step of lowering a glass transition temperature of said coating by allowing said coating to flow and deform during compaction without breaching during sintering process, then protecting the integrity of the coating. 43) A method for using the low loss, high magnetic saturation magnetic material as defined in any one of claims 1-18 in a power system.
